title: LLM enabled generative collaborative design in a mixed reality environment

agents.description: LLM-based multi-agent simulating the Engineer stakeholder role, responsible for assessing material choosing, technical feasibility and structural integrity in design proposals.
LLM-based multi-agent simulating the Designer stakeholder role, responsible for defining product appearance, usability, and overall user experience.
LLM-based multi-agent simulating the Merchant stakeholder role, responsible for setting product direction, analyzing the market, and defining product price.
LLM-based multi-agent simulating the Consumer stakeholder role, responsible for offering end-user reactions, focusing on usability, functionality, and satisfaction with the proposed design.

Core LLM orchestrator that analyzes design sessions, transforms them into design semantics, generates design proposals, executes quantitative evaluation, and produces specialized prompts for GAI tools (DALL-E 3, MVDream).

orchestration.orchestration_pattern_description: The system uses a multi-agent collaboration pattern where an LLM orchestrator coordinates multiple role-specific agents (Engineer, Designer, Merchant, Consumer) in an iterative self-collaborative design mechanism. The orchestrator decomposes design tasks, distributes them to specialized agents for evaluation, collects scores via a quantitative evaluation method, and iterates until a satisfactory design proposal is produced. The output then feeds into GAI tools (DALL-E 3 for 2D, MVDream for 3D) in the H2M stage.

multi_agent_coordination_strategy.multi_agent_coordination_strategy_descri: LLM-based multi-agents representing four stakeholder roles (Engineer, Designer, Merchant, Consumer) cooperate to evaluate and refine design proposals through simulated design sessions. Each agent contributes domain-specific evaluations weighted by stakeholder importance at each design stage. The orchestrating LLM aggregates scores and drives iterative refinement until an adaptive threshold is met.

human_integration.human_integration_description: Humans initiate the process by providing design instructions and review feedback. In the M2M stage, LLM-based multi-agents autonomously simulate stakeholder discussions. In the H2M stage, actual stakeholders review GAI-generated 2D and 3D conceptual schemes, provide feedback for iteration, and interact with the MR environment (physical 3D-printed model augmented with AR projections). In the H2H stage, stakeholders collaborate throughout the entire design process supported by AI-generated artifacts and the MR environment.

retrieval.application_domain_description: Collaborative product design process involving multiple stakeholders (consumers, designers, engineers, merchants) for developing physical products, demonstrated through a children's heart education product case study.
retrieval.problem_description: In collaborative design, diverse stakeholder backgrounds cause misunderstandings and communication barriers, leading to delays in design delivery and decreased creativity. Existing technologies (MR/AR/VR and AI) applied independently have limitations: AI-generated objects lack detailed visual information, and MR requires significant time to create realistic virtual objects. There is also insufficient precision in GAI-generated visual schemes due to lack of specificity in design prompts.
retrieval.proposed_solution: The AI-augmented Multimodal Collaborative Design (AI-MCD) framework integrates LLM, Generative AI, and Mixed Reality technologies across three collaboration stages: (1) Machine-to-Machine (M2M) using LLM-based multi-agents to simulate stakeholder roles, generate design proposals, and produce specialized prompts via an iterative quantitative evaluation mechanism; (2) Human-to-Machine (H2M) using GAI tools (DALL-E 3 for 2D, MVDream for 3D) and MR (3D printing + AR) for immersive prototype review; and (3) Human-to-Human (H2H) collaboration enabled throughout by the M2M and H2M outputs.
retrieval.research_methodology_description: Two prototype systems (M2M and H2M collaboration prototypes) were developed and validated through a case study involving the design of a science-education product on the human heart for children. The case study demonstrated the framework's workflow and compared modeling efficiency between professionals and novices using traditional vs. AI-MCD approaches.

decision_quality_improvement: LLM-based multi-agents demonstrated professional expertise and logical coherence in design discussions, producing refined and enhanced design proposals through internal collaborative sessions.
Comparative analysis across multiple product types showed multi-agents' ability to enhance and detail designs through iterative internal collaborative design sessions.
The quantitative evaluation method with stakeholder weight assignment and sentiment semantic scoring enables objective selection of the best design proposal.
response_time_impact: Modeling efficiency improved by 500% for professional users (from 10 hours to 2 hours average).
Modeling efficiency improved by 1000% for novice users (from 30 hours to 3 hours average).
The framework significantly reduces time and resource costs in the collaborative design process.

ce_technique_effectiveness.iterative-reasoning: The iterative prompting mechanism enables LLM to refine design proposals through multiple rounds until an adaptive threshold score is met, producing more precise and professional design prompts for GAI.
ce_technique_effectiveness.dynamic-decomposition: Decomposing the collaborative design process into M2M, H2M, and H2H stages, and further into functional phases (design semantics transformation, proposal generation, quantitative evaluation), clarifies responsibilities and reduces collaboration complexity.
ce_technique_effectiveness.in-context-learning: Predefined rules, standards, and stakeholder role definitions embedded in prompts enable LLM-based multi-agents to simulate domain-expert behavior without fine-tuning.

failure_modes: 3D GAI generation overlooks detail information such as product materials, functions, and colors, requiring manual refinement.
AI-generated design objects face limitations in presenting detailed visual information such as product size and structure.
limitations: The 3D model generation still requires significant effort to produce detailed 3D models.
The self-collaborative design mechanism does not eliminate the need for human interaction entirely; more research is needed on human-machine interaction in GAI design.
Future work is needed to develop novel evaluation and iteration methodologies extending self-collaborative design scope to 2D or 3D for more precise and comprehensive evaluation.

baseline_methods: Traditional modeling (manual 3D modeling by professionals and novices)
identified_research_gaps: More research attention is needed for human-machine interaction in the GAI design field
Much effort is still needed in the generation of detailed 3D models

Future work may aim to develop novel evaluation and iteration methodologies, extending the self-collaborative design's scope to 2D or 3D for more precise and comprehensive evaluation and iteration of design proposals
